coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots: A promising nanocarrier for bioimaging and drug delivery in cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Jana, Pradip
Dev, Abhimanyu
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon quantum dots have emerged as a promising nanomaterial for simultaneous bioimaging and drug delivery in cancer due to their excellent properties like fluorescence emission, good photostability, high water solubility, good biocompatibility, small size, ease in surface functionalization and low toxicity. The current review discusses the different synthetic routes, post synthesis modifications, optical properties and characterization techniques for carbon dots. Further, problems associated with cancer chemotherapy and the potential nanomaterial-based strategies to overcome it, have been highlighted. Besides, the recent progress in the domain of carbon dots-based strategies in anticancer drug delivery and real time monitoring through fluorescence imaging are also discussed. In continuation, approaches like carbon dots based active and passive targeting, tumor microenvironment targeting, multifunctional targeting, etc. are elaborated. Carbon dots applications for in vivo imaging and biodistributions in cancer models are also collectively presented. Finally, critical opinions and future scopes in the development of carbon dots based advanced nano-theranostics in cancer treatment are discussed. The current review can offer broader vision and a sound roadmap to the researchers working in the domain of cancer nano-theranostics and cancer targeted drug delivery.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots: A promising nanocarrier for bioimaging and drug delivery in cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences & Technology, Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, Ranchi, Jharkhand 835215, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences & Technology, Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences & Technology, Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, Ranchi, Jharkhand, 835215, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon quantum dots have emerged as a promising nanomaterial for simultaneous bioimaging and drug delivery in cancer due to their excellent properties like fluorescence emission, good photostability, high water solubility, good biocompatibility, small size, ease in surface functionalization and low toxicity. The current review discusses the different synthetic routes, post synthesis modifications, optical properties and characterization techniques for carbon dots. Further, problems associated with cancer chemotherapy and the potential nanomaterial-based strategies to overcome it, have been highlighted. Besides, the recent progress in the domain of carbon dots-based strategies in anticancer drug delivery and real time monitoring through fluorescence imaging are also discussed. In continuation, approaches like carbon dots based active and passive targeting, tumor microenvironment targeting, multifunctional targeting, etc. are elaborated. Carbon dots applications for in vivo imaging and biodistributions in cancer models are also collectively presented. Finally, critical opinions and future scopes in the development of carbon dots based advanced nano-theranostics in cancer treatment are discussed. The current review can offer broader vision and a sound roadmap to the researchers working in the domain of cancer nano-theranostics and cancer targeted drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or carbon dots (CDs) are comparatively a new member of carbon nanomaterial family [1,2]. These small sized (less than 10 nm) carbogenic fluorescent nanoparticles came into limelight after accidental finding by Xu et al. in 2004 and thereafter, a completely new area of nanoparticle research with wide range of applications was opened [3]. In general, CDs are rich in elements like carbon, oxygen and hydrogen. Several additional elements like nitrogen, sulphur, boron, etc. can be doped through appropriate methods to alter CDs properties [4]. Besides, CDs possess good water solubility and tunable fluorescence property. Usually, presence of carboxyl functionality over CDs surface contributes to its good water solubility. While, functionalization with other chemical groups and surface passivation of CDs can be easily made to modify the fluorescence behavior, reduce toxicity and alter physical properties [2,4]. Moreover, CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, possess good photostability and these can be synthesized easily in cost effective manner which facilitate their multiple applications in the field of optoelectronics, bioimaging, photocatalysis, biosensors, and drug delivery [1,5,6].

In recent times, CDs have been hugely utilized by researchers in cancer cell imaging and cancer targeted drug delivery. Several reviews are available in relation to the synthetic schemes, optical behaviors and versatile applications of CDs [1,7–9]. But there is no such review comprehensively dedicated to drug delivery applications of CDs in cancer. In cancer a vast number of side effects arise in patients due to non-specific delivery of drug molecules [10]. Thus, improvement of targeting efficiency towards cancer cells has become one of the major challenges in the cancer chemotherapy.

In this regard, our interest is focused towards the contributions of CDs in the area of anticancer drug delivery and real time tracking as a novel nanomaterial with desired surface functionalities, minimal toxicity and biocompatibility. CDs have gained tremendous attention as a suitable nanocarrier material for cancer specific drug delivery due to their smaller size and effortless conjugation facility provided by surface functional groups like carboxyl and amino groups [2,10]. Conjugation of CDs with specific ligands (e.g., folic acid, transferrin, etc.) of overexpressed cancer cell surface receptors helps the drug-carrier-ligand conjugate to navigate and gain access into cancer cells through receptor mediated endocytosis [11,12]. Whereas, tumor extracellular microenvironment triggered targeting, which can prevent recognition of targeting ligands by endothelial system and their subsequent clearance from blood circulation, is getting much attention in recent times. In this strategy, targeting ligands are protected inside a furtive layer, reach tumor site through enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect and get exposed at a suitable tumor extracellular environment like acidic pH and redox potential, enabling their attachments with cancer cell specific receptors [13]. It is expected that improvement in targeting specificity in such ways will be able to reduce overall drug dosage, enhance drug bioavailability and reduce side effects [14,15]. Besides, use of CDs in drug delivery can improve solubility, half-life and accumulation of drug candidates inside the cancer cells [12]. Further, fluorescence property of CDs can be utilized to combine bioimaging with drug therapy to visualize in vivo drug distribution and drug release through monitoring the fluorescence emitted by CDs [1,4]. In in vivo imaging, CDs with long wavelength emission is desired to avoid the interference with fluorescent signal of cells and tissues [16]. Fluorescence property of CDs also make their characterization process simple and easy [10]. Thus, in this review after a short description of synthetic approaches and optical properties of CDs, we will concentrate on different strategies employed for targeting cancer cells to deliver drugs selectively, monitoring the movements of delivery systems using CDs, their current scopes and future prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic approaches and properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Several methods are reported for CDs synthesis and numerous materials have been used as carbon precursors. Synthetic methods are categorized as ‘top-down’ and ‘bottom-up’ approaches [17]. ‘Top-down’ approaches involve breaking down of large carbonaceous materials into smaller ones through methods like arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, and laser ablation while ‘bottom-up’ approaches involve conversion of small organic molecules into desired sized CDs through different routes viz. hydrothermal or solvothermal methods, microwave assisted pyrolysis, ultrasonic wave mediated reactions and template/supported synthesis [18–21]. Different top-down and bottom-up approaches are collectively presented in Fig. 1.

Some modifications in the synthesized CDs are required to obtain CDs of uniform size, desired surface functionalities, and sometimes to regulate optical properties. Size uniformity is a very crucial aspect for several properties of synthesized CDs. Surface functional groups over CDs contribute to its solubility property, conjugation etc. Surface passivation and heteroatom doping are the approaches reported to improve the optical behavior of CDs [4,7]. These modifications in CDs can be done during synthesis or may be achieved after the synthesis [22,23]. The synthetic approaches, during synthesis modifications, post synthesis modifications and optical properties of CDs are discussed in detail in subsequent sections.

As earlier mentioned, Xu et al. first discovered an unfamiliar fluorescent carbon nanomaterial which later became popular as CDs during purification step of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) synthesis using arc-discharge method. In this method, arc shoot was oxidized using nitric acid for attachment of carboxyl groups to improve water solubility. Then sodium hydroxide solution of pH 8.4 was added to the obtained sediment for the formation of a stable suspension. The obtained suspension provided different carbon nanomaterials such as SWCNTs, short tubular carbons and fluorescent CDs when separated by gel electrophoresis method. The CDs further differentiated into several components that emitted fluorescence of multiple colors depending on their sizes and elution patterns under UV excitation at 365 nm [3].

Sun and his group first demonstrated CDs synthesis via laser ablation method. They created a carbon target through hot-pressing a combination of powder graphite and cement under argon flow. A neodymium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet (Nd:YAG) laser (1064 nm, 10 Hz) was employed for ablation while keeping the prepared carbon target in the flow of argon gas supported with water vapor at 900 °C and 75 kPa. Then, the produced CDs were refluxed for 12 h in aqueous nitric acid medium. Further, the acid treated CDs were surface passivated with organic polymers, such as diamine terminated oligomeric poly-(ethylene glycol) (PEG1500 N) and poly-(propionylethyleneimine-co-ethyleneimine) (PPEI-EI), which have shown strong photoluminescence. For surface passivation, the polymer was mixed with CDs sample and heated at 120 °C for 72 h. When the reaction was completed, the reaction mixture was dispersed in water after cooling at room temperature, and then centrifuged to obtain purified CDs. The size of CDs was around 5 nm and quantum yields (QYs) were reported from 4% to more than 10% at 400 nm excitation [24].

In another study, Hu et al. reported single step fluorescent CDs synthesis from suspension of carbon materials in organic solvents via laser ablation method. A Nd:YAG pulsed laser was utilized to irradiate carbon material suspended in organic solvents like diethanolamine or PEG200 N through ultrasonication. At the end of the exposure to laser, residues present in suspension were precipitated through centrifugation while supernatant containing CDs were collected [25]. Recently, Xu et al. synthesized specific surface functionalized CDs using femtosecond (fs) laser ablation technique on graphite powders dispersed in aminotoluene liquid. In this method, desired surface functionalization can be achieved by controlling the nature of dispersion medium. A Ti: sapphire fs laser beam with a wavelength of 800 nm was implemented for ablation. Finally, large graphite particles were separated by centrifugation (1000 rpm for 10 min) and impurities were removed by dialysis (molecular weight (MW) cutoff = 1000) to obtain final CDs [26].

Electrochemical synthesis of CDs become popular due to simple methodology and easily available equipment since it was first described by Zhou et al. [32]. This method of CDs synthesis requires an electrode which could serve as a carbon source and an electrochemical cell. Usually, electrochemical reaction takes place in an applied potential which lead to the formation of CDs [33,34]. Zhao et al. reported CDs synthesis via electrochemical oxidation of graphite column electrode as carbon source in presence of saturated calomel electrode and Pt wire counter electrode in an aqueous solution of sodium dihydrogen phosphate (0.1 M). The dark brown coloration of the transparent solution ensures CDs synthesis. Further, centrifugation of the obtained solution removes the unwanted larger particles if present and size separation of CDs present in supernatant liquid was done using centrifugal filter device. The photoluminescence (PL) property of the synthesized CDs indicated its dependency on the size of CDs and the emission maxima at 445 nm and 510 nm were found for CDs of 1.9 and 3.2 nm sizes, respectively [33].

Yao and his co-researchers used an anode of graphite ring and a cathode of a titanium tube separated by an insulated O-ring, kept inside an electrolyzer containing sterilized water as an electrolyte medium. Upon simultaneous application of ultrasonic power and electrolytic voltage CDs of 2–3 nm size were rapidly obtained eliminating the complex process of purification. The resultant CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence property and photostability and a quantum yield (QY) of 8.9% [35]. Here, the current density plays a significant role in determining size and emission wavelength of CDs. CDs of larger size and longer wavelength can be obtained by reducing the current density [36]. Recently, Hou et al. synthesized CDs in one step electrochemical method using sodium citrate and urea as a precursor. They employed Pt sheets as the electrodes and applied 5 V direct current (DC) potential for about 1 h [28].

In general, bottom-up methods gained much popularity over top down methods due to their convenient, rapid, cost effective, simple and eco-friendly nature to synthesize fluorescent CDs without use of harsh conditions and toxic reagents [1]. Synthesis of CDs from different sources via bottom-up methods, their passivating or doping agents and their properties are summarized in Table 1.

Hydrothermal and solvothermal treatments are commonly used, cost effective and eco-friendly methods to synthesize CDs [1]. Number of carbon sources like ascorbic acid [37], citric acid [38], glucose [39], soy milk [40], orange juice [29], coffee beans [41], tea leaves [42] etc. were used to synthesize CDs via hydrothermal method. Usually, in hydrothermal treatment, carbon precursor containing aqueous solution is reacted inside a hydrothermal chamber at a temperature above boiling point of water [1]. Treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine at 150 °C for 2 h via hydrothermal method yielded nitrogen doped fluorescent CDs with a high QY of 58.6%. The prepared CDs displayed excitation independent emission behavior and excellent photostability [43]. More recently, Wang et al. prepared CDs, by heating catechol in ultrapure water inside autoclave at 200 °C for 24 h. The resulted CDs have shown QY of 32% without any doping and post processing [44].

Solvothermal carbonization involves heat treatment of carbon precursors in high boiling point organic or non-aqueous solvents [1]. Chen et al. employed one-pot solvothermal reaction using p-phenylenediamine as a precursor and isopropanol as a solvent which produced excitation independent red responsive nitrogen doped CDs. The reactants were kept inside a teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave and treated at 200 °C for 1 h inside an oven. The emission band for the synthesized CDs was found in between 560 and 700 nm where the peak was centered at 605 nm [45]. More recently, for very first time, Multicolor emission CDs with cyano functional group was prepared from inorganic salt ammonium carbonate [(NH4)2CO3] via solvothermal treatment (160 °C, 12 h) in chloroform and diethylamine solvent mixture [31]. Implementing a green synthetic approach, our group has also synthesized fluorescent CDs through solvothermal treatment of a plant Buchnania lanzan leaf extract in a teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave at 160 °C for 4 h inside an oven [46].

Microwave assisted synthesis is a rapid and the most convenient method to synthesize CDs [21]. In recent times, several groups have reported CDs synthesis with high QY utilizing microwave irradiation with different resources like- L-ascorbic acid and β-alanine [47], mixture of citric acid, L-cysteine and dextrin [48], L-glutamic acid [49], citric acid and cyclen [50], histidine [51]. Liu et al. synthesized CDs from denatured protein in aqueous solution. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), pepsin or lipase were used as protein and were denatured in presence of denaturing agents like urea or guanidine hydrochloride. The aqueous solution of protein and denaturing agent was kept inside a microwave oven (700 W) and treated for about 5 min. The obtained brown crude solid was dissolved in ultrapure water and afterwards centrifugation was done at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Further the supernatant was dialyzed (MW cut off = 8000–14000) for 48 h to remove excess precursors and unwanted materials. The surfaces of prepared CDs were rich in hydroxyl and amino functionalities. The as prepared CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence, QY up to 14% depending on the precursors and these can serve as temperature nanosensor and as pH probe [52].

Zhang et al. developed nitrogen doped hydroxyl group functionalized CDs with ultrahigh fluorescence QY (99%) via microwave treatment (less than 5 min) of citric acid with ethanolamine or tris(hydroxylmethyl)aminomethane [53]. It was pointed that 1:1 molar ratio of reactants results in highest hydroxyl and graphitic nitrogen content which reflects in high QY of prepared CDs. The prepared CDs size and PL property via microwave method also depend on the heating time. Longer heating duration results in larger size of CDs and their PL exhibition at longer wavelength. In a study, CDs of average sizes (2.75 ± 0.45) and (3.65 ± 0.6) nm were achieved by treating reactants for 5 and 10 min, respectively [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: During synthesis and post synthesis modifications

ce:section.ce:para: A major challenge arises during CDs synthesis is the control of size uniformity that is crucial for optical and other characteristics [17]. This can be overcome through several approaches during preparation or post-preparation treatments. CDs of uniform size can be achieved via template or confined pyrolysis of organic precursors using nanoreactors [54,55]. The obtained size in confined pyrolysis largely dependent on the texture of employed nanoreactor. Porous silicas are generally used as nanoreactor, which can serve as hard template to prevent aggregation of prepared CDs as well as offers easy removal [56,57]. Polymers unstable at high temperature also can serve as nanoreactor to provide confinement in the cases where carbon yielding sources are thermally cross linkable polymers [58]. Other than the size control during preparative stages, number of post-synthesis treatments like centrifugation [33], gel-electrophoresis [59], ultrafiltration [60] and dialysis [52] can be employed to obtain CDs of uniform size.

Surfaces of CDs typically contain functional groups like carboxyl which facilitate their water solubility as well as provide a convenient support for further surface modifications by different approaches such as covalent conjugation [14,61], coordination [62],
π − π
interactions [63], etc. Alteration of surface structural attributes of CDs can significantly change the emission wavelength of prepared CDs [64]. Amino-terminated agents like ethanolamine and polyethylene glycol (PEG1500 N) are used as surface passivating agents to enhance the PL property. Non-luminescent CDs prepared from graphite source when passivated with PEG, exhibited strong PL property ( Fig. 2) [24]. However, surface passivation sometimes may increase the overall size of CDs, which limits their applications [17].

Heteroatom doping is used to regulate the optical and photocatalytic properties of CDs. Nitrogen (N) doping [38,65–67] is the most popular approach to improve the QY of CDs by changing the chemical and electronic structure. For example, in a study N-doped CDs were synthesized from ammonium citrate ( Fig. 3). Here, ammonium citrate molecules initially went through molecular dehydration to get aromatized. Further, the intermediates were carbonized to form carbon nuclei of N-doped CDs. Finally, the carbon nuclei forms nanocrystalline N-doped CDs with different functional groups at the surface. Several other doping elements such as sulfur (S) [68,69], phosphorus (P) doping [70,71] and boron (B) doping [72,73] have also been reported to improve the optical characteristics of CDs. Further, co-doping [74,75] strategy also has been employed to improve fluorescence property. CDs produced from citric acid and L-cysteine (source of N and S atoms) by hydrothermal treatment have resulted in N and S co-doping with QY of 73% [76].

Even though there are diversity in the structures of CDs, they exhibit some common optical properties with respect to absorption and photoluminescence [7]. In the following subsections the common optical properties of CDs are discussed.

Optical absorption spectrum of CDs is typically seen in UV region with an edge spreading into visible region (
Fig. 4b). The peak found in the wavelength range of 230–270 nm is assigned to π - π * transition of C = C bond and a peak shoulder observed in the range of 300–330 nm may be attributed to n - π * transition of C = O bond [77,78]. For example, nitrogen doped CDs produced from one step femtosecond laser ablation method have exhibited absorption peak at 264 nm with an edge spreading to about 290 nm [26]. CDs obtained in a hydrothermal method have shown characteristic absorption peaks exactly at 264 and at 330 nm [79].

The most exciting property of CDs is photoluminescence (PL). Although after years of research, the actual mechanism for the origin of PL is not clear. Several research groups suggested the dependence of emission wavelength on the excitation wavelength (λex) [80,81]. The probable reasons for PL phenomenon explained from time to time includes, size dependent optical selection (quantum effect), versatile emissive traps due to surface defects on CDs, surface passivation, diversity in surface functionality, and surface states induced by heteroatom doping [82–86]. However, in recent years number of cases with λex independent emission behavior of CDs have been reported [33,58,87]. This may be due to the consistent nature of surface state, uniformity in size distribution of CDs and generation of organic fluorophores [1,86,88]. Although, there are differences in opinions by different groups. Wang et al. reported λex independent emission behavior of nitrogen doped CDs prepared by pyrolysis of poly(acrylonitrile)- poly(methyl methacrylate) (PAN@PMMA) polymeric core-shell nanoparticles. Here, the PL property of the prepared CDs depends on the pyrolysis temperature [58].

Another exciting optical property of CDs is the up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL). The UCPL of CDs is seen mainly because of the multi-photon activation. Here, concurrent absorption of two or more photons leads to shorter emission wavelength as compared to the excitation wavelength of light. Several research groups synthesized CDs where UCPL property is reported [89,90]. This UCPL behavior having excitation wavelength at near-infrared region (NIR) and emission wavelength at visible region found several advantages in the field of bioimaging having increased tissue penetration depth at NIR, reduced chances of autofluorescence and photodamage of imaging cells. It is also useful in metal ion detection and for effective catalyst design [1,90,91].

In characterizations of CDs, several unique properties of CDs like distinct peaks in the range of 200–400 nm in UV absorption, excitation dependent fluorescence emission (usually), small particle size range (below 10 nm), characteristic broad peak (2θ in between 20° and 30°) in X-ray diffraction (XRD) are generally utilized [92] , [93] , [94]. Morphological study of CDs can be done with the help of Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) or High-resolution TEM (HRTEM). TEM images can give comprehensive idea of particle size, size distribution and shape of CDs [95,96]. The TEM image of PEG surface passivated CDs synthesized by microwave treatment is presented in Fig. 4a. The TEM image suggested successful synthesis of CDs with surface coating of PEG and the particle size is found below 10 nm [97]. Besides, Atomic force microscope (AFM) is also used to get the idea of size and shape of the CDs [98,99].

The inset image of CDs solution in Fig. 4b showing excellent fluorescence under UV light. In majority cases, CDs shows excitation dependent emission behavior (Fig. 4c) [92]. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy is used to determine surface functional groups of CDs. In general, CDs exhibits a broad peak centered in the range of 3300–3400 cm−1 for stretching vibrations of -OH and -NH groups, a peak in between1700–1720 cm−1 for CO stretching, a peak at about 1400 cm−1 for C-N stretching and a peak in the range of 1390–1500 cm−1 for CC (aromatic) [92–94,100]. X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) can indicate the elements present in CDs. Primarily, CDs are consisting of elements like carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), Sulphur (S). Deconvolution of the elements at high resolution XPS could suggest the different groups present in CDs [92,98,101]. Raman spectrum of CDs can indicate the extent of disorders in its structure. Raman spectrum of CDs generally shows an intense D-band at about 1350 cm−1 and a low intensity G-band at about 1580 cm−1. The ratio of intensity of D-band and G-band (ID/IG) measures the extent of defects [93,100].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cancer chemotherapy problems and their potential remedies

ce:section.ce:para: Among different cancer treatment approaches, chemotherapy is the major one to combat cancer [102]. However, conventional chemotherapeutic drugs most often face problem of non-specific distribution throughout the body [103]. Besides, multi drug resistance (MDR) is another challenge in cancer chemotherapy. MDR enable cancer cells to escape from the cytotoxic effects of the anticancer drugs by developing resistance against them during or after completion of therapy [104]. Hydrophobicity of some anticancer drugs also brings difficulty in the development of suitable delivery systems [105,106].

Conventional chemotherapeutic drugs are distributed throughout the whole body via blood stream. The non-specific delivery of chemotherapeutic drugs shows cytotoxicity towards malignant cells but at the same time causes serious damage to the normal cells of the bone marrow, lymphoid tissue, gut, hair follicles, etc. These unintended cellular damages lead to some common side effects in cancer patients including hair loss, pale skin, vomiting and many more.[10]. Toxicities towards normal cells limit the dose and frequency of anticancer drugs resulting in compromised efficacy. For example, cardiotoxicity detected in treatment with popular anticancer drug doxorubicin limits its therapeutic usage [107].

Drug resistance developed by cancer cells is a crucial aspect for the effectiveness of chemotherapy. The cellular mechanism of MDR involves overexpression of plasma membrane P-glycoproteins (P-gp). The P-gp efflux the drugs from the cells and thus causes decreased sensitivity of drugs and decreases their intracellular accumulation. This effect is more distinct with drugs which follow passive diffusion for the entry into cells through lipid bilayers. After the entry, these types of drugs bind with P-gp, which leads to the ATP dependent conformational changes of the channels to efflux them out of the cell [108] , [109]. P-gp can efflux various anticancer drugs like doxorubicin [110], paclitaxel [111], etc. Overexpression of P-gp is a common feature of most of the acquired MDR in tumors [109].

One more critical obstacle in cancer chemotherapy is that most of the anticancer drugs are hydrophobic in nature. As the anticancer drugs can harm the normal cells also, it is preferred to deliver through intravenous route. To develop intravenous formulations aqueous solubility is an important aspect, although it is very uncommon among anticancer drugs [105]. To improve the solubility of anticancer drugs additives such as cremophor EL (polyoxyethylated castor oil), dehydrated alcohols etc. are used but these agents cause serious side effects including hypersensitivity reactions, neurotoxicity, cardiotoxicity, nephrotoxicity and peripheral neuropathy [108]. Thus, the advances in the construction of novel delivery systems for hydrophobic anticancer drugs excluding the use of organic solvents has received great attention. In this respect, nanoparticle based anticancer drug delivery can be a promising alternative.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential strategies for targeted anticancer drug delivery
ce:section.ce:para: Attempts are made to develop nanocarrier based drug delivery systems which can target cancer cells while sparing normal cells and can provide effective localization of drugs at cancer site with therapeutic concentrations to subdue the complications associated with chemotherapy [112]. Thus, nanocarrier based anticancer drug delivery can reduce toxicity and maximize therapeutic efficacy [108]. Besides, nanoparticle systems have the ability to overcome from MDR and to provide effective therapeutic benefit [113]. Interestingly, some characteristics of cancer cells and surroundings can create opportunities for nano-drug delivery systems to precisely target cancers, which includes tumor microenvironment, various overexpressed receptors (e.g., transferrin, folate, CD44, integrin etc.) on cancer cells and vasculatures [113,114].

Some formulation related parameters like particle size, surface charges, hydrophilicity, hydrophobicity and covalent attachment of ligands to carrier systems specific for overexpressed receptors also have big contributions towards the development of cancer targeted nano delivery systems [108].

Consideration of tumor microenvironment (TM) is also a very important aspect while designing a new delivery system. Various exclusive conditions like vascular abnormalities, hypoxia, acidic pH, angiogenesis, etc. are observed only in tumor tissues, which can be utilized to design newer strategies [114]. These newer strategies in cancer treatment advance the conventional chemotherapy and also at the same time aim to overcome drug resistances.

During the continuous growth of the tumor cells newer blood vessels formation (angiogenesis) takes place to meet the huge demand of nutrients and oxygen. A number of biological signals are involved in the initiation of angiogenesis process and the process is very chaotic, results in poorly vascularized areas. The newly formed vessels are found leaky which cause the enhanced permeability of small molecules passing through the vessels and promote their entry into the interstitium of tumor cells ( Fig. 5) [115]. In addition, lymphatic system of tumors is not well organized and thus drugs that get access through leaky vasculature may have the long retention period in tumor interstitium (EPR, Enhanced permeability and retention effect) [116]. This EPR concept is vastly utilized over the last two decades in the design of tumor selective drug delivery strategies [117,118]. However, EPR based drug delivery systems targeting tumors must be long circulating to provide sufficient accumulation in the target site. The most promising way to extend the circulation period of nano-carriers is to coat their surface with some hydrophilic polymers like polyethylene glycol (PEG). The masking of drug carriers with PEG leads to the delay in the surface binding of opsonin proteins and subsequent clearance by mononuclear phagocytic systems (opsonization process) [119].

Tumors exhibit low extracellular pH as compared to normal cells. This acidic extracellular pH arises mostly because of high lactate production through glycolysis pathway in hypoxic cancer cells. Further fermentation of the lactate produces significant numbers of free protons which are eliminated from the cells to maintain the intracellular pH. Increase in the levels of protons in the extracellular space develops an acidic environment [120,121]. The acidic extracellular pH is reported to promote metastasis and develop resistance against many anticancer drugs by increasing the expression of p-glycoprotein [122,123]. This acidic environment can be altered in such a way that it no longer remains responsible for the metastasis or drug resistance. Besides, this unique acidic pH environment can be strategically used to design pH sensitive nanocarrier based delivery systems which could only be activated upon reaching this target site [112]. This approach of pH dependent targeting could improve the selectivity and efficacy of chemotherapeutic drugs.

Hypoxia is a hallmark feature of the tumor microenvironment where oxygen supply to the cells become insufficient mainly because of aberrant angiogenesis and impaired vascular function [124]. As earlier discussed, to meet the high demand of oxygen in rapidly proliferating cells, neighboring cells of tumor release multiple growth factors which in turn develop new blood vessels. However, as these new vessels are poorly developed and leaky in nature, they fail to meet the need of oxygen having poor perfusion [122]. Hypoxic areas in tumors get very less exposure of chemotherapeutics as the delivery of drugs in these areas are limited through the vasculature [125]. Hypoxia is also involved in genotype selection which favors the tumor growth. In addition, it is reported to induce tumor metastasis and invasiveness by potentiating different signaling pathways. Besides, hypoxic condition can interrupt the DNA repair mechanisms and cause genomic instability [124]. These conditions may lead to the development of resistance towards chemotherapeutic drugs [112].

Although, hypoxia can serve as a therapeutic target in cancer treatment. It is responsible for the stabilization of hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF-1α), which causes activation of genes that regulate tumor cell proliferations and drug resistance development [126]. Thus, inhibition of HIF-1α can be an effective approach in the suppression of tumors. Besides, nanoparticle-based strategies can also be employed to develop hypoxia responsive drug delivery systems (drug release under hypoxic condition). For an example, azobenzene a hypoxia sensitive bio reductive linker was used to develop a protective coating over a nanocarrier carrying siRNA for the delivery in tumor. Upon entering into the hypoxic environment of tumor protective layer is cleaved from the nanocarrier surface due to break down of the linker, allowing the cellular internalization of the system [127].

The development of new blood vessels from the existing blood vessels is known as angiogenesis. Tumor induced angiogenesis takes place in several steps which comprises activation of endothelial cells, destruction of the basement membrane, migration of the endothelial cells, new vessel generation and remodeling of the developed vessels. In rapidly growing tumors hypoxic condition promotes transcription of the hypoxia inducible factor (HIF) that can potentiate different proangiogenic factors like- vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), platelet derived growth factor (PDGF), basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF), transforming growth factor-α (TGF-α), interlukin-8 (IL-8), etc [128,129]. After activation, endothelial cells can secrete proteolytic enzymes like matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) to degrade the basement membrane and extracellular matrix. The migration of endothelial cells through extracellular matrix is regulated by the interaction between transmembrane αvβ3 integrin receptor expressed by endothelial cells and extracellular matrix proteins (vibronectin, fibronectin, etc.). Further, the internal layer of endothelial cells leads to apoptosis to form new vessel lumen. In the final step immature vasculature is remodeled which is further supported by pericytes and smooth muscle cells [130]. The ability of the tumors to form new blood vessels is a crucial attribute in the spreading of cancer cells throughout the body (a process called metastasis) [131]. Thus, anti-angiogenic approach can become an area of focus for cancer therapeutics. Anti-angiogenic therapy by targeting tumor neovasculature has some major advantages over conventional therapies: (a) due to tumor specific process targeting it possesses little systemic toxicity, (b) with endothelial cell targeting, the problem associated with drug to reach tumor cells crossing many layers from the vasculature can be eliminated, (c) endothelial cells are less likely to undergo genetic variations, minimizing the chance of drug resistance as found in conventional cancer therapies [132,133]. The main angiogenic targets which are currently explored for nanoparticle based drug delivery includes vascular endothelial growth factor receptors (VEGFRs), matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), αvβ3 integrins, etc.,[113].

There are number of receptors overexpressed by tumor cells. Some specific ligands show strong affinity towards these overexpressed receptors on cancer cells. Targeting ligands are conjugated on the nanoparticle surface for recognition by specific receptor expressed at the target site and this strategy of targeting cancer cells is called active targeting. Targeting ligands can be antibody fragments, monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) or non-antibody ligands (e.g., folate, cRGD, transferrin, etc.) [113,114]. After ligand-receptor interaction, the nanoparticles are internalized through endocytosis ( Fig. 6). The receptor mediated cellular uptake has shown increased therapeutic benefits of the nanoparticle drug delivery systems [93,134].

The advancement in nanotechnology in recent years unfolded huge opportunities for the development of rational anticancer drug delivery systems [135]. The emerging potential of nanotechnology lies in the properties like flexibility in the development of customizable nano constructs having facility of controllable surface modification, accommodation of multiple payloads, etc [136]. Thus, nanoparticulate systems provide the facility to construct delivery systems which can prevent, detect and treat resistant cancer cells while at the same time reduce serious side effects and improve the solubility and stability of anticancer agents. Nano delivery systems of size ranging from 1 to 100 nm can move into the tumor site through leaky tumor vasculature via EPR effect. Multi-functionalization of nanoparticles can improve their circulatory period and thus prolong the exposure of chemotherapeutic drugs. Thus, nanostructure with these unique properties could effectively improve the intracellular accumulation of anticancer drugs into cancer cells and subsequently could improve their therapeutic efficacy [109].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs in cancer targeting and bioimaging
Targeted drug delivery and monitoring approaches with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-based tumor specific drug delivery and monitoring can be achieved through several approaches including passive targeting [137], active targeting [138], targeting tumor microenvironment [13,139], antigen-antibody interactions [140], targeting through aptamers [141] or by combining several attributes as a multifunctional targeting [142]. CDs based different tumor targeted delivery and imaging systems which are described in this section are summarized in Table 2.

Passive targeting involves the movement of nanocarriers into the tumor surroundings and cells by diffusion or convection process utilizing the leaky vasculature of tumors. The accumulation of nanocarriers in the tumor surrounding and cells is facilitated by the EPR effect [115]. However, to efficiently deliver drugs using passive targeting, nanocarriers should possess some important properties: (a) the size of the nanocarriers desired to be in between about 10 and 100 nm. It is usually considered that nanocarrier size needs to be kept below 400 nm for the efficient movement through the leaky tumor vasculatures. To improve circulation half-life, nanocarrier size is generally expected to be higher than 10 nm as kidneys are usually capable of filtering particles smaller than 10 nm in greater extent [143]. Smaller nanoparticles (<5 nm) are cleared faster than larger nanoparticles. For example, renal clearance efficiency of gold nanoparticles increased from 4% of injected dose (ID) to 52.5% of ID 24 h after injection, when size of the nanoparticles decreased from 6 nm to 2.4 nm [144]. To evade from the capturing by liver the size of the nanocarrier should be ideally below 100 nm. (b) Kidneys can filter positively charged particles. Thus, the nanocarriers have to be either anionic or neutral to escape the elimination by kidney. (c) The nanocarriers should have the ability to evade the reticulo-endothelial system to avoid opsonization followed by phagocytosis process [143].

Considering the above aspects, CDs of desired size can be synthesized and utilized as a suitable carrier system in targeting of cancer cells and surroundings or as an imaging probe. Bao et al. synthesized fluorescent CDs of 2–5 nm in diameter from citric acid and urea via solvothermal method. The synthesized CDs were found nontoxic against HeLa cells, 4T1 cells, HepG2 cells and effectively reached at the H22 tumor region after intravenous administration. The buildup of CDs is visualized by near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence imaging. The effective buildup of CDs in tumor region is expected via EPR effect due to its very small size. However, strong NIR fluorescent signal is also obtained from kidneys at 30 min post injection which gradually decreased until disappearing after 24 h, suggesting rapid accumulation and subsequent clearance of CDs from the kidney [137].

Active targeting in cancer can be achieved by molecular recognition of cancer cells via the ligand-receptor interactions [158]. CDs can be conjugated with specific ligands of overexpressed receptors of cancer cells to allow preferential accumulation of CDs carrying drug inside the cancer cells [159]. As earlier mentioned, CDs have several functional groups on their surface, this property can be utilized to conjugate targeting moieties. Several works are already reported implementing this strategy, by conjugating targeting ligands over CDs for cancer cell specific delivery. Some of these CDs-based receptor targeted delivery systems are discussed below.

The folate receptor is a cell surface receptor which is anchored to the cell membrane by a glycolipid anchor glycosylphosphatidylinositol [160]. Folate receptor is found overexpressed on the most of cancer tissues. Besides, its presence is limited in normal tissues making it as a promising target for anticancer drugs. It is overexpressed in cancers like epithelial, cervical, ovarian, lung, breast, colorectal, kidney and brain [161,162]. Folic acid, the natural ligand of folate receptor, is a non-immunogenic water-soluble vitamin [163,164]. Folic acid can remain intact over a broad range of pH and temperatures, and retain its affinity towards folate receptor after conjugation with drug molecules [161]. On successful attachment of folic acid to the folate receptors located on the cell surfaces of cancer tissues, it is internalized through endocytosis. However direct conjugation of folic acid to a drug can result in reduced affinity of folic acid towards folate receptors or may alter the therapeutic efficacy of the drug [163]. Thus, for folate receptor targeting a suitable nanocarrier material is essential for the development successful cancer targeting delivery system.

When folic acid was attached with CDs for the targeted delivery of anticancer drugs, it exhibited less toxicity towards normal cells and showed great potential in specific killing of cancer cells. Doxorubicin conjugated CDs when targeted with folic acid towards HeLa cells, it showed reduced toxicity and higher killing rate due to folic acid mediated specific internalization and drug release in the acidic pH of tumor cell ( Fig. 7I). The folic acid mediated internalization of CDs was confirmed by fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells. The folic acid conjugated CDs treated cells exhibited bright green fluorescence whereas cells treated with CDs only showed no fluorescence (Fig. 7II) [93]. Folic acid when chosen as a precursor to prepare nitrogen dopped CDs, also showed excellent targeting ability towards folate receptor expressed on malignant cells [145].

Recently, in our lab at Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, India we have synthesized folic acid conjugated-anticancer drug capecitabine loaded passivated (with PEG) CDs (CAP-FA-CDPs) for folic acid mediated active targeting of colorectal cancer. Cellular uptake capacity of the developed CAP-FA-CDPs and CDPs systems were analyzed by fluorescence imaging of human adenocarcinoma (HT-29) cell line, 8 h after treatment. The strong blue fluorescence emitted by CAP-FA-CDPs treated cells ( Fig. 8B) and negligible fluorescence in CDPs treated cells (Fig. 8A) suggest the significant cellular uptake of CAP-FA-CDPs mediated by interactions between targeting ligand folic acid and folate receptor decorated on the cancer cells surface (Unpublished work).

Integrins are well known cell surface receptors which enable mechanical attachments between cell and extracellular matrix and provide bidirectional cellular signaling [165]. The αvβ3 integrin receptor is overexpressed in tumor endothelium and tumor cells thus it attracts much attention of researchers. Arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD) peptide sequence is frequently used to target integrin receptor in tumor vascular endothelium to suppress angiogenesis and metastasis. The specific affinity of RGD sequence and integrin receptor has been widely employed to develop different cancer specific drug delivery systems using nanocarrier materials [166].

Recently, Feng et al., developed carbon dots-based pH/redox dual responsive tumor microenvironment triggered targeted anticancer drug delivery system where RGD peptide molecule plays the role of a targeting ligand. The developed system has the ability to expose RGD peptide at the tumor extracellular pH and it has exhibited efficient tumor uptake via RGD-αvβ3 integrin (ligand-receptor) interaction [13].

CD44 receptor has been recognized as one of the promising cell surface biomarkers for metastatic cancer and cancer stem cells [167]. CD44 receptors are overexpressed in several cancer cells such as intestinal cancers, colon cancers, leukemia, prostate cancers, breast cancers and pancreatic cancers [168]. Hyaluronic acid (HA) a natural linear polysaccharide has been reported to have specific interaction with CD44 receptor [169]. HA is an extracellular matrix component and it is non immunogenic, biocompatible and biodegradable in nature [170]. HA can efficiently accumulate inside cancer cells via interaction with CD44 receptor and it can easily convert into small fragments by lysosomal enzyme hyaluronidase-1 which is abundant in the tumor microenvironment [171]. Because of these unique features, HA has been used as a targeting ligand in various nanoparticle systems. HA modified nano carriers have displayed excellent targeting capacity and therapeutic benefit in comparison to free chemotherapeutic drugs in various in vitro and murine tumor models [172,173].

Duan et al. developed HA modified carbon dots based multifunctional nano delivery system where HA is used to construct CD44-HA specific affinity for cancer targeting ( Fig. 9I). HA was anchored on CDs surface by classical EDC/NHS reaction. The developed CD-HA-Hep/DOX system exhibited significant cytotoxicity (around 85%) towards human gastric cancer cells- MGC-803 cells after 48 h of treatment (Fig. 9II.A). However, the same system exhibited minimal cytotoxicity (about 20%) after 48 h of treatment on normal gastric epithelial cells (GES cells) (Fig. 9II.B). The results suggest the minimal toxicity of the developed system towards normal cells and effective selectivity towards cancer cells [146]. Similarly in another work, HA modified carbon dots-doxorubicin drug conjugate exhibited HA-CD44 mediated internalization by 4 T1 cells. The accumulation of developed system inside 4 T1 cells was ensured using confocal laser scanning microscopy and flow cytometry analysis. Compared to free doxorubicin, HA modified nano system exhibited superior in vivo antitumor efficacy in 4 T1 cell tumor models [147].

Transferrin is a glycoprotein mainly found in blood plasma which is produced majorly from liver and also from some other organs. Transferrin primarily transports iron with in the vascular system. Overexpression of transferrin receptors on many cancers cells surface makes transferrin a potential cancer targeting ligand. Thus, in recent past in various drug delivery systems, transferrin has been utilized as a targeting ligand for the selective accumulation inside cancer cells [174,175]. Anabousi et al. reported significantly higher level of transferrin receptors in epithelial cells of lungs with lung cancer in comparison to normal epithelial cells. Here, conjugation of transferrin with liposomes, enabled an excellent system to deliver cytotoxic drugs to the lung cancer sites [176]. Li et al. reported permeability of transferrin conjugated carbon dots across the blood brain barrier (BBB) via endocytosis facilitated by transferrin receptor in in vivo zebrafish model. As transferrin can efficiently gain access across the BBB, it shows great promise in the areas of brain cancer targeting. When transferrin conjugated carbon dots system was investigated for doxorubicin delivery in pediatric brain tumor cells, in vitro study exhibited much superior accumulation of conjugated system at the brain tumor cells as compared to doxorubicin alone. Further, the carbon dots-transferrin-doxorubicin conjugate was found with higher cancer cell killing capability than doxorubicin alone resulting in decrease in pediatric cancer cell viability by 14–45% [14]. Most recently, Hettiarachchi et al. covalently conjugated carbon dots with transferrin and anticancer drugs epirubicin and temozolomide to develop a triple conjugated delivery system for glioblastoma brain tumors ( Fig. 10). In in vitro studies, transferrin conjugated carbon dots-drugs system was found with significant tumor killing capacity to glioblastoma tumor cell line SJGBM2, as compared to carbon dots-drugs system and free drug mixtures [12].

CDs which are having small size and large surface area, can be strategically utilized to develop tumor microenvironment targeted systems for the selective and efficient anticancer drug delivery. These systems can target tumor extracellular acidic pH, upregulated proangiogenic growth factors, etc [139]. Zhou et al. developed a nanocomposite system of mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) with carbon dots attached on its surface and loaded with doxorubicin (DOX). Bright green fluorescence was observed in in vitro imaging of HeLa cells treated with carbon dots-MSNs nanocomposite after 2 h of incubation ( Fig. 11I.b), while cells incubated without nanocomposites exhibited no fluorescence (Fig. 11I.a) suggesting the cellular imaging potential of CDs capped nanocomposite system. Further, the nanocomposite system exhibited almost 80% DOX release at mild acidic pH (pH 5.0) due to dissociation of system as compared to pH 7.4, at which only 15% DOX was released ((Fig. 11II.a). In vitro cell viability study of HeLa cells suggested remarkably higher cytotoxicity of CDs-MSNs-DOX nanocomposite as compared to DOX and MSNs-DOX (Fig. 11II.b) which could be due to DOX release inside HeLa cells subsequent to internalization of CDs-MSNs-DOX nanocomposites [148]. Recently, Seo et al. developed DOX loaded pH sensitive carbon dots (DOX-pSCDs) for the colon cancer targeting. Here, DOX encapsulation was facilitated by imidazole group situated over carbon dots. The release profile of DOX-pSCDs exhibited up to 80% DOX release in pH 6.5 after 3 days. While only 10% of DOX release was seen in pH 7.4 after 3 days. Moreover, DOX-pSCDs showed rapid release when pH of buffer was changed from pH 7.4–6.5. This quick release occurred due to protonation of imidazole in pSCDs at acidic pH which enables fast release of DOX [149].

Cell nucleus has a significant role in proliferation, differentiation, and cell metabolism [177]. Genetic materials are found in the nucleus. Thus, the cell nucleus has been considered as a critical site for interaction with anticancer agents, for destruction of genetic materials and subsequent killing of tumor cells efficiently [178,179]. Jung et al. developed a unique nucleus targeting zwitterionic carbon dots for drug delivery in cancer. Carbon dots were decorated with zwitterionic groups through a single step synthesis in presence of β-alanine which acts as a passivating and zwitterionic group. β-alanine facilitates entry of carbon dots into cytoplasm and further into nucleus. When this carbon dots-based delivery system was conjugated with doxorubicin (DOX) by non-covalent interaction, it exhibited significant in vivo antitumor effect in 4T1-luc2 breast cancer bearing mice due to enhanced tumor accumulation and nuclear delivery. The tumor accumulation of CDs-DOX was confirmed by in vivo and ex vivo imaging [150]. Recently, fluorine doped carbon dots (FCDs) were prepared by solvothermal process from tridecafluoro-1-octanol and poly-(ethyleneimine), having nucleus targeting ability. Further, anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) and boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dye were used to develop nanocomposites of FCDs-DOX and FCDs-BODIPY, respectively. When FCDs were tested in human cervical cancer cells (HeLa), these were significantly found inside HeLa cell nucleus, suggesting effective nucleus directing capacity of FCDs. Whereas, carbon dots without fluorine doping were mainly found to distribute in the cytoplasm [180].

Delivery into subcellular components in cancer can provide greater efficacy even at a low dose, with reduced adverse effects and reduced incidence of multidrug resistance. Multidrug resistance is minimized or overcome as the efflux of therapeutic agents from the subcellular organelles is quite tough [181]. In eukaryotes, golgi apparatus is an vital organelle for different functions like biosynthesis, secretion and intracellular delivery of several large molecules [182]. Biomolecules rich in cysteine moieties like galactosyltransferase and protein kinase D are used to specifically target Golgi apparatus [183]. Li et al. synthesized blue chiral fluorescent probe where L-cysteine was used to modify carbon dots with Golgi apparatus targeting ability. Studies revealed that the chiral stereochemistry and free sulfhydryl groups of L-cysteine play a significant part in golgi apparatus targeting [151].

Mitochondrion is also a major organelle in eucaryotic cells, responsible for producing ATP and regulating cell metabolism [184]. Mitochondrion is mainly characterized by high negative potential and strong alkalinity in its physiological environment [183]. There are two common strategies of mitochondria targeting probes- first, modifying fluorescent probes with triphenylphosphonium (TPP), the most common targeting unit of mitochondria [185,186]. Second, designing lipophilic cationic probes containing amine such as quaternary ammonium salt, pyridine, rhodamines or other structures [187–189]. TPP is a mitochondrion targeting small molecule, so it is an effective design strategy to graft TPP on the surface of CDs. Wu et al., conjugated amine rich CDs with TPP for preparing CDs-TPP fluorescent probe ( Fig. 12I). Here, to check the mitochondria targetability of the prepared fluorescent probe, mito-tracker (mitochondria specific staining fluorescent dye) was used as a contrast and for co-staining of MCF-7 cells along with CDs-TPP nanoprobe. In the confocal fluorescence imaging of MCF-7 cells (Fig. 12II), red fluorescence given by mito-tracker and yellow fluorescence produced by nanoprobe were found overlapping which suggests the successful mitochondria targeting by CDs-TPP nanoprobe [152]. CDs with lipophilic cation also have mitochondrion targeting properties owing to the transmembrane potential of mitochondria. Hua et al. selected raw material with amino groups and positive charges (ethylenediamine and mercaptosuccinic acid) to prepare mitochondrion targeting CDs. The benzene ring structure of CDs combined with a large number of amino groups and positive charge make CDs lipophilic in nature and of delocalizing positive charge species, which endues CDs with good mitochondrion targeting properties [190].

Lysosome is a cellular organelle that plays important role in several cellular processes like- degradation and recycling of cellular wastes, cell signaling, cell membrane repairing, autophagy, cell apoptosis, etc [191]. Such cellular processes can be monitored by lysosome imaging using fluorescent nanomaterials. Chen et al. prepared CDs with amine terminated surface and with bright green fluorescence emission behavior. The weakly basic amine terminated surface endows CDs with acidotropic effect which facilitates its accumulation in the acidic lysosome and excellent fluorescence imaging [153].

Lipid droplet (LD), an organelle responsible for lipid storage and plays a vital role in different metabolic events. She et al. prepared boron and nitrogen co-doped CDs (BNCDs) from 3-aminophenylboronic acid by a solvent free carbonization method for tracing and monitoring of movements of LD. The synthesized BNCDs exhibited long wavelength fluorescence emission and was found useful in wash free and real time LD imaging without use of any LD-targeting ligands [192].

Cancer cells can be targeted and visualized using cell specific antibodies conjugated over fluorescent carbon dots [193]. For example, cetuximab antibody (C225) can specifically interact with epithelial growth factor (EGF) receptor overexpressed by several cancers like lung cancer, head and neck cancer. Su et al. synthesized iodine doped carbon quantum dots (I-CQDs) and functionalized their surface with PEG diamine (PEG 1500 N) to obtain terminal amine groups containing PEG coated nanodots (I-CQD-NH2). Further, they conjugated cetuximab with I-CQDs-NH2 (I-CQDs-C225) with the use of EDC-NHS coupling ( Fig. 13A) and carried out in vitro assessment in lung cancer cell lines- HCC827 cells (with high expression of EGFR), H23 (with low expression of EGFR), and HLF cells (with low expression of EGFR) and performed imaging with confocal laser microscope. The HCC827 cells displayed intense fluorescence (Fig. 13B), suggesting the ability of cetuximab-conjugated I-CQDs to specifically reach and accumulate in cancer cells having overexpression of EGFR. To analyze subcellular localization of I-CQDs-C225 in HCC827 cells, laser scanning confocal microscopy was performed using a LysoTracker® Green DND-26 staining agent. The blue fluorescence obtained from I-CQDs-C-225 (Fig. 13C) was found perfectly overlapping with the green fluorescence emitted by LysoTracker, indicates that I-CQDs-C-225 were localized mainly in the lysosomes of HCC827 cells [140]. Several cancer cell specific antibodies like CA125 antibody in human ovarian cancer cell (OVCAR-3) [194], CEA8 antibodies in HeLa cells [195], trastuzumab in HER-2 positive breast cancer cells [196] can be used for molecular targeting in the respective cancers. The antibodies can be conjugated with drug-carbon dots complex for the development of cancer specific drug delivery.

Aptamers are single stranded DNA or RNA oligonucleotides in nature and these have high affinity and specificity towards a target molecule [154]. A lot of attention is attracted by aptamers as targeting agents owing to their chemical stability, non-immunogenicity, easy synthesis, and wide range of applicability [141,197]. Besides, these oligonucleotide molecules can develop well organized forms retaining the strong affinity and specificity towards target molecule[197]. Aptamers are well established as tumor recognition elements for cancer targeting of anticancer drugs [155]. Aptamers can readily distinguish between cancerous and healthy cells at molecular level [198]. Zavareh et al. developed chitosan-carbon dots-aptamer (CS-CDs-Apt) nanoparticle system for specific targeting of anticancer drug 5-fluorouracil in breast cancer treatment. MUC-1, a glycoprotein which is highly overexpressed in breast cancer and 5TR1 aptamer is reported to have specific binding affinity to this glycoprotein. Here, in this study, the specific affinity of 5TR1 aptamer towards MUC-1 glycoprotein is utilized for cancer cell internalization and therapeutic efficiency [154]. Recently, AS1411 aptamer conjugated and CDs encapsulated Fe3O4 metal organic framework (MOF) core shell nanostructure was developed ( Fig. 14A). The Fe3O4 @MOF-CDs-Apt nanocomplex has the potential in pH-responsive targeting of anticancer drug DOX and fluorescence imaging at the same time in triple negative breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231). The Fe3O4 @MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocomplex interacts with nucleolin highly expressed over MDA-MB-231 cancer cells and transports DOX into the nucleus of these cancer cells. The AS1411 aptamer-nucleolin interaction mediated selective delivery of DOX into the cancer cells leads to the killing of more than 77% cancer cells after 24 h of nanocomplex incubation (Fig. 14B). The targeting ability and site-specific delivery of the developed system was also monitored by fluorescence imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells. In fluorescence imaging bright green fluorescence was exhibited by CDs or red fluorescence seen for DOX (Fig. 14C) and the perfect overlapping of these images indicate the efficient internalization of the developed system [155].

Gene therapy has the potential to cure number of acquired diseases and inherited disorders. The important aspect for successful gene therapy is to effectively deliver therapeutic genes into the targeted cells with the help of proper vector [199]. In gene delivery, synthetic non-viral vectors have multiple advantages such as easy production and alteration, absence of host immunogenicity as compared to recombinant viral vectors [200]. Although, toxicity and physicochemical stability related issues limit the clinical application of synthetic vectors [201,202]. In such scenario, nontoxic, biocompatible and chemically stable nanocarrier carbon dots can be a used for the development of effective gene delivery system [203,204]. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), a frequently used non-viral vector, contains a large number of amino groups in the structure. Liu et al. used PEI as surface passivating agent during the synthesis of CDs with enhanced fluorescence property and gene delivery ability. Here, the outside layer of cationic polymer helps to facilitate DNA transfection and the CDs in the inner layer can emit obvious fluorescence ( Fig. 15I), which can serve as bioimaging agent. The developed CDs-PEI exhibited good transfection of pDNA in COS-7 (African green monkey kidney cell) and as well as in HepG2 cells (human hepatocellular liver carcinoma cells). Further, when transfected COS-7 cells were imaged using laser scanning confocal microscopy (Fig. 15II), strong fluorescence was emitted by CDs-PEI accumulated in cytosol, whereas no fluorescence was seen in case of the untreated cells (negative control), indicating successful cellular uptake of the CDs-PEI-pDNA complex [156]. Wu et al. developed a small interfering RNA (siRNA) based theranostic nanocomplex for concurrent use in diagnosis and therapy of lung cancer. Fluorescent CDs produced via microwave treatment of glycerol and PEI serves the purpose of cancer cell diagnosis. Next, the PEI is attached onto PEI-CDs via disulfide linkage, which forms reducible PEI-CDs (rPEI-CDs). The electrostatic interaction between positively charged rPEI and negatively charged siRNA molecules leads to the rPEI-CDs-SiRNA complex formation. Folate conjugated on the outermost layer of the complex serves as a tumor targeting ligand. When the nanocarrier system is uptaken by cancer cells, the link between PEI and SiRNA is detached by glutathione and then therapeutic siRNA is released inside the cytosol. Enhanced accumulation of the developed nano-system was observed in lung cancer cells as compared to normal fibroblasts. The nano-delivery system significantly decreases tumor growth nearly up to 30% in H460 tumor bearing mice model. Further, the system also effectively inhibited the gene expression of cyclin B and epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) genes [157].

Incorporation of both diagnosis and chemotherapy features in a single multifunctional system enables real time and non-invasive monitoring of movement and release of therapeutic molecule in cancer treatment [205]. Sachdev et al. produced silver-zinc oxide nanocomposite (NC) covered with carbon dots as a multifunctional system (CDs-Ag@Zno), where, Ag@Zno component is expected to evoke cancer cell apoptosis while CDs were used as a fluorescent imaging probe. The movement of CDs-Ag@Zno nanocomposite was tracked with the help of green fluorescence emitted by CDs. The in vitro studies of developed nanocomposite system on MCF-7 and A549 cancer cells suggested concentration dependent cytotoxicity [94].

In another work, doxorubicin (DOX) loaded theranostic nanogel was prepared by crosslinking CDs with folate-terminated PEG modified hyaluronic acid (FA-PEG-HA). Due to extraordinary fluorescent property of CDs, the developed system is able to non-invasively track the movement of chemotherapeutic molecule (DOX) in real time. The nanogel exhibited a perfect DOX release in weak acidic media (55% release in pH 5.0 within 38 h) however in neutral pH only 14.7% release is seen, demonstrating pH-controlled drug release behavior of the system. The cellular uptake and cytotoxicity study in human ovarian cancer cell SKOV3, illustrated maximum DOX release and accumulation in the nucleus and effective cancer cell killing is attributed to HA and FA dual receptor facilitated drug targeting [142].

Recently, Deng et al. developed a cluster of self-assembled oil soluble CDs into water soluble CDs and further constructed a nanocomposite of CDs-cluster-DOX/cRGD polyacrylic acid (PAA)-octyl amine (OA) for bioimaging and tumor targeted drug delivery ( Fig. 16A). The role of amphipathic polymer (cRGD-PAA-OA) is to alter surface charge and to improve active targeting ability of the system. Tumor targeting of the constructed nanocomposite was investigated in vitro by incubation with αvβ3 integrin receptor overexpressed U87MG glioma cell line and MCF-7 breast cancer cell line. In confocal laser scanning microscope imaging, stronger green fluorescence in U87MG cells treated with CDs cluster/cRGD-PAA-OA compared to MCF-7 cells or U87MG cells treated with CDs cluster/PAA/OA (Fig. 16B), suggests enhanced cellular internalization of CDs cluster/cRGD-PAA-OA nanocarrier through endocytosis mediated by cRGD. In MTT assay, CDs cluster-DOX/cRGD-PAA-OA nanocomposite expressed greater cytotoxicity compared to DOX in both U87MG and MCF-7 cells (Fig. 16.C). However, the cellular growth inhibition was better in case of U87MG cells compared to MCF-7 cells, supports the cellular uptake results of effective internalization of nanocarrier mediated via αvβ3 integrin receptor overexpressed by U87MG cells. In in vivo antitumor activity study of U87MG tumor bearing mice, the nanocomposite system effectively suppressed tumor volume as compared to DOX only [138].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Treatment strategies in different cancers with CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Lung cancer is one of the most frequent cancers, which is heterogeneous in nature and possesses significant threat to human health [206]. CDs being an attractive drug delivery vehicle and bioimaging agent, holds immense potentials in the development of novel theranostic systems which could offer real time tracking of the nanocarrier system and at the same time could provide the desirable therapeutic benefit. Yang et al. prepared CDs by chemical oxidation of oligoethylenimine and β-cyclodextrin in phosphoric acid and further assembled CDs into nanocomplexes with hyaluronic acid for lung cancer targeting. Further, the nanocomplexes were encapsulated with DOX, which exhibited statistically significant cancer cell killing capacity than DOX only in H1299 lung cancer cells. The enhanced cytotoxicity of DOX-loaded nanocomplexes is expected due to their HA mediated cellular uptake as compared to passive diffusion of DOX in lung cancer cells [207].

Sachdev et al. developed a chitosan-based hydrogel (HY) containing fluorescent CDs and anticancer drug 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) was loaded in it for lung cancer targeting. Here, CDs were entrapped in hydrogel matrix via ionic crosslinking and 5-FU was encapsulated with the help of hydrophobic interaction to develop 5-FU@CDs-HY system. This engineered strategy to develop a dual functionalized hybrid assembly offers a common platform for tracking of cellular internalization, release pattern of the drug and cellular alteration in response to the drug. Apoptotic potential of the developed 5-FU@CDs-HY assembly was evident viewing the changes in cell cycle distribution in A549 human lung adenocarcinoma cells [208].

Recently, Wu and his group reported nitrogen enriched CDs (NCDs)-based DNA nanocomplex ( Fig. 17). DNA nanotechnology possesses programmability and precisely controlled architecture to develop a well-defined nanostructure. For bioimaging and anticancer therapy the outstanding fluorescence and photodynamic characteristics of NCDs were utilized in DNA/NCDs nanostructure. NCDs directs DNA nanoprism (NPNCD) to self-assemble at a high temperature and pH range. When NPNCD conjugated with KRAS siRNA for KRAS mediated non-small cell lung cancer therapy, the system unveiled outstanding gene delivery capacity and impressing in vitro anticancer effect [209].

Breast cancer represents one of the most prevalent type of cancers in women globally. Therapeutic payload release in a controlled manner at the target site using nanocarriers can improve the therapeutic efficacy and reduce the related toxicity [210]. Rich expression of proteins such as P-glycoprotein (P-gp/ABCB1) and breast cancer resistant protein (BCRP) causes multi drug resistance (MDR) which is a key obstacle in breast cancer treatment [211]. Anticancer drugs can be delivered to breast tumors using CDs as a nanocarrier through passive or active targeting. Controlled release of drugs from CDs have been achieved to obtain desired therapeutic efficacy and overcome the MDR.

D’souza et al. prepared fluorescent CDs taking vancomycin as a precursor by hydrothermal method and loaded with flutamide (FLU) on their surfaces which serves as a good candidate for the transport and release of the drug candidate in tumor microenvironment (at acidic condition, pH 5.2). The drug conjugated CDs exhibited effective intracellular drug accumulation in MCF-7 cells. In a cell viability study, CDs showed excellent cytocompatibility. When treated with FLU-CDs at 100 µg/ml concentration, only 8% MCF-7 cells survived after 72 h of treatment. Further, at the same FLU concentration, both FLU-CDs and FLU showed similar cytotoxicity, suggests non-interference of CDs in therapeutic activity of FLU. The outcomes indicated good potential of vancomycin derived CDs as a drug carrier for anticancer drug delivery in breast cancer [212]. Kong and his group prepared CDs from citric acid and ethylenediamine and conjugated with DOX. CDs-DOX complex exhibited a pH dependent drug release behavior, releasing drug in the acidic environment of tumor and showed improved killing capacity of MCF-7 cells than DOX only. Further, more DOX delivery into the cell nucleus is seen from CDs-DOX conjugate as compared to free DOX [213]. Li et al. synthesized hyaluronic acid (HA) modified CDs-DOX nanocarrier system for the specific targeting of breast cancer cells. HA here act as CDs component and at the same time serves as the active targeting ligand having specific binding affinity towards CD44 receptor overexpressed on breast cancer cells. In vitro MTT assay exhibited high cytotoxicity potential of HA-CDs-DOX complex on 4T1 cells. Superior in vivo antitumor efficacy of HA-CDs-DOX system was obtained as compared to free DOX in 4T1 cell tumor model ( Fig. 18) [147].

Brain cancers are the significant reason of cancer related mortality globally. In human, gliomas are the most dominant and fatal type of brain tumors [214]. Zheng et al. reported a novel carbon dot (CD-Asp) derived from D-glucose and L-aspartic acid pyrolysis, which is having self-targeting capability towards C6 glioma cells. In vivo fluorescence imaging ( Fig. 19A) exhibited verry fast buildup of CD-Asp (5 min after tail vein injection) in the brain site, suggesting its free penetrating ability across blood-brain barrier (BBB). The maximum intensity at brain site was detected after 15 min of administration and gradual reduction in intensity over time suggests systemic elimination of CD-Asp. The three-dimensional reconstruction image (Fig. 19B) taken 20 min after injection, showed much stronger fluorescence intensity in the glioma region as compared to usual brain tissue, confirming the precise accumulation of CD-Asp in the glioma region. Subsequently ex vivo imaging (Fig. 19C) confirms the earlier obtained results [215]. Gao et al. fabricated CDs with maleimide-polyethylene glycol-amino succinimide succinate (Mal-PEG-NHS) linked RGERPPR peptide which is involved in tissue imaging and brain gliomas targeting. Here, RGERPPR acts as a tumor penetrating peptide, which enters tumor tissue and further moves to glioma. Mal-PEG-NHS acts as a linker to attach anticancer drugs [216]. Transferrin receptors are expressed on BBB and often overexpressed on the brain tumor cells [14]. Thus, transferrin molecule can be used to cross BBB and to target brain tumors. Transferrin conjugated CDs for DOX delivery to target pediatric brain tumors, where transferrin receptor mediated endocytosis is utilized to cross BBB and enter inside brain tumor cells [14] and transferrin conjugated CDs for dual drug epirubicin and temozolomide delivery in glioblastoma brain tumors [12] have already been discussed under sub Section 4.1.2.4.

In worldwide scenario, liver cancer ranks third in the death counts caused by different cancers and at the same time the incidence rate is increasing very fast [217] , [218]. With the fast-growing nano science to achieve improved drug delivery, for controlled release and for imaging of drug movement in liver cancer, carbon dots based many strategies have been implemented. CDs possesses high potential as a nanocarrier in liver cancer due to their unique properties like smaller size, specific charge useful in passive targeting [219] and presence surface functional groups advantageous in active targeting [220]. Li et al. reported fluorescent CDs from fresh tender ginger juice by hydrothermal treatment having high inhibitory potential against human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (HepG2) while minimal toxicity in ordinary liver cells (FL83B). The synthesized CDs can bring HepG2 cell apoptosis by triggering the pro-apoptotic factors and can effectively inhibit tumor growth in mice model. The effective accumulation of CDs at the tumor site is expected via EPR effect [221].

Zeng and his group conjugated green-emitting CDs with anticancer drug DOX by electrostatic interaction which is pH sensitive and hence can differentiate between cancer cells and normal cells and selectively release drug at the pH of cancer cells. During in vivo estimation of HepG2 tumor bearing mice model (liver cancer model), the CDs-DOX conjugate demonstrated enhanced drug efficiency and exhibited suitability of fluorescent CDs as an imaging probe [222].

In a different study, Folic acid (FA) was utilized as a targeting moiety for fluorescent CDs based nanocarrier for DOX (FA-CDs-DOX) delivery and bioimaging in liver cancer. In in vivo model, after 20 days of treatment, FA-CDs-DOX complex showed 72.13% tumor inhibition while DOX exhibited only 41.95% inhibition ( Fig. 20I.a). The tumor sizes of FA-CDs-DOX and CDs-DOX treated groups were obvious smaller than the control group (Fig. 20I.b). In the in vivo fluorescence imaging, FA-CDs-DOX exhibited fluorescence (Fig. 20II) and reflected the outline of tumor site. Further, ex vivo imaging of main organs and tumor tissue (Fig. 20III) showed strong fluorescence in heart, liver and kidney and low intensity fluorescence in small part of tumor tissue in case of treatment with DOX. While in treatment with FA-CDs-DOX, intense fluorescence was seen in tumor tissue and quite low intensity fluorescence was detected in liver and kidney, confirming the excellent targeting capacity of FA-CDs-DOX complex [220].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging and biodistribution study in cancer models using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The significant aspect in cancer chemotherapy is to develop a drug delivery system that can be tracked in real time to understand its biodistribution for the specific delivery of drugs at the cancer cells avoiding normal cells. In this respect, carbon dots possess impressive potential in bioimaging due to their properties like excellent fluorescence, presence of surface functional groups for easy modification, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [223]. In vitro imaging application of CDs based nanocarriers in cancer cells are collectively presented in multiple articles [223,224] , [7] and this is also discussed in earlier sections of our review. However, as per our knowledge there is no such review article which comprehensively presented the in vivo imaging and biodistribution studies carried out in recent times using CDs in cancer animal models. In vivo imaging and biodistribution can elucidate the targeting ability and facilitate real time tracking of the developed delivery systems. Here in Table 3, we have presented CDs synthesized from various precursors in different methods and their in vivo imaging in different cancer models.

He et al. injected fluorescent CDs synthesized using citric acid and ethylenediamine in HeLa tumor bearing mice and performed in vivo fluorescence imaging to investigate the imaging ability of the developed CDs. Intense fluorescence was exhibited by CDs at the tumor site (red circle), 30 min after injection, despite of strong autofluorescence background in body tissue ( Fig. 21I.a) and a gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity at tumor area was noted up to 6 h. At the same time intense fluorescence was seen in the bladder (white circle) indicating efficient renal clearance of CDs.
