Further, strong fluorescence at tumor tissue was also obtained in ex vivo imaging and biodistribution study of CDs (Fig. 21I.b) which confirmed the imaging capability and tumor uptake of the CDs [226].

Zheng et al. prepared oxaliplatin conjugated highly luminescent CDs (CDs-Oxa) and subcutaneously injected in tumor bearing Kunming male mice. After injection, initially CDs-Oxa was found to concentrate at the injection site which was indicated by strong fluorescence from the tumor area (Fig. 21II.b) and afterwards fluorescence spreads and a gradient intensity pattern was developed at the injection site whose center was at the tumor (Fig. 21II.c). Further, fluorescence intensity was reduced suggesting the distribution of CDs-Oxa to other organs or elimination from the body (Fig. 20II.d-e) [228].

Zhao and his group prepared CDs functionalized with hyperbranched polyamidoamine polycation (HPAP-CDs) and a PEGylated outer shield (m-PEG-polyethyleneimine copolymer with dimethylmaleic acid; PPD) which can cleave at a specific pH for the delivery of pDNA (the whole system abbreviated as: PPD@HPAP-CDs/pDNA). The tracing and targeting ability of the developed system was assessed by in vivo imaging in MCF-7 tumor containing mice. Administration of PPD@HPAP-CDs/pDNA into mice through intratumoral injection exhibited strong fluorescence from tumor area (Fig. 21III.a). Intravenous administration of the developed system displayed wide spread fluorescence in whole body with noticeable background fluorescence because of skin autofluorescence. Although high fluorescence still seen at the tumor site suggesting that fluorescence property of CDs used in the system leading to the tumor imaging. Further, tumor targeting and distribution of the developed systems in different organs visualized by ex vivo imaging (Fig. 21III.b). The PPD@HPAP-CDs/pDNA group showed stronger emission from tumor tissue. Strong fluorescence seen in liver tissue indicates that the elimination of CDs could be by liver [229].

Recently Bao et al., synthesized near-infrared (NIR)-emitting CDs and injected in H22 tumor bearing mice through intravenous route and examined in vivo tumor accumulation and biodistribution via NIR fluorescence imaging (NIR laser wavelength 655 nm). Initially after injection of CDs, whole body of mice exhibited strong NIR fluorescence. At 3 h post injection, only tumor area displayed strong NIR fluorescence whereas intensity in the whole body was decreased noticeably (Fig. 21IV.a) suggesting the effective accumulation of CDs at the tumor site which can be attributed to EPR effect. When ex vivo imaging of main organs was done, strong NIR fluorescence was seen in kidney (Fig. 21IV.c) indicating the elimination of CDs via kidney [137].

CDs has become a fascinating member in the world of nanomaterials due to its excellent fluorescence behavior, good biocompatibility, environment friendly, low cost and easy synthesis schemes. CDs has tremendous scope in the development of future nanodevices. However, there are much scope for improvements in the understanding of several fundamental aspects of CDs. The specific synthesis mechanism is not clear and precise control over size, surface functionality and properties are still not possible [232]. There is also a lack of systematic and scalable synthesis methods which will provide CDs with required morphology, optical and other properties [233]. Photoluminescence (PL) is the most fascinating property of CDs. However, the mechanism responsible for generation of PL property is still in debate [234]. Even the particular structure of CDs is still unclear due to lack in systematic structural characteristic measures related to determination of structural features [21]. The future research should be engaged in revealing the possible mechanism how the synthesis determines the structure and several other attributes like PL property of CDs. Generally, synthesized CDs are found to exhibit low QY and Fluorescence in the short wavelength (green or blue) region which is found unsuitable for in vivo imaging. Much emphasis should be given to develop high QY CDs and with fluorescence emission in the red/ near IR spectral region for effective tissue penetration in in vivo imaging [6]. Besides, research should be encouraged on doped and co-doped CDs having multicolor emissive properties [159]. For long term imaging purpose, it is also desirable that CDs should have higher intracellular photostability [232].

In CDs based anticancer drug delivery a number of scientific reports have been published which employed the strategy of passive targeting, active targeting via single receptor and single drug, utilization of acidic environment of tumor, etc. ( Fig. 22). However, there are a lot of scopes for the development of CDs based futuristic strategies for more profound cancer targeting, controlled drug release and reversal of drug resistance ( Fig. 23). Many hallmark features of cancer like hypoxia, angiogenesis etc. are nearly unexplored while developing CDs based anticancer drug delivery systems. Hypoxic condition of tumor microenvironment could be utilized to develop hypoxia sensitive CDs based polymeric systems which could deliver anticancer drug specifically at the hypoxic tumor microenvironment. Hypoxia sensitive linker could be used for polymer coating over drug loaded CDs, on reaching hypoxic tumor microenvironment linker molecule will be detached and it will expose the inner drug loaded CDs. Another CDs based approach could be the targeting of angiogenesis process. Some major targets like VEGFRs, MMPs, αvβ3 integrin could be explored for CDs based antiangiogenic therapy. The main advantage of angiogenic blood vessels targeting is that, it excludes the problem associated with cancer cell targeting where drug has to cross many layers from the vasculature to reach cancer cells. Further, the chance of genetic variations in vascular endothelial cells are very less which minimize the incidence of drug resistance.

A newer approach of multifunctional targeting incorporating several smart features like targeting ligand, suitable polymer etc. in a single delivery system could be employed for more effective cancer targeted drug delivery and tracing. Nanogels or nanocomposites with CDs could be developed where the nanoarchitecture of suitable polymer provide the arrangement for drug entrapment and its controlled release while the fluorescent CDs acts as a tracing agent. Simultaneous use of multiple ligands like one for cancer cell targeting and other for tumor neovasculature targeting could be done. The strategy of use of more than one drug in a single delivery system could be employed for synergistic effects and to tackle drug resistance.

Cancers like lung cancer, liver cancer, breast cancer, and brain cancer are mainly focused while very few works of carbon dots are reported in other cancers like ovarian cancer, cervical cancer, colorectal cancer, prostate cancer, etc. Therefore, a lot need to be explored in these areas.

In vitro imaging of cellular uptake of CDs based drug delivery systems are widely reported but very less number of reports are available for in vivo imaging of CDs based systems and their biodistributions in animal models. The in vivo real time tracking of drug delivery systems could provide conclusive evidence on the successful development of CDs based cancer targeting drug delivery systems. Moreover, systematic research should be carried out on toxicity and metabolic pathways of CDs in in vivo models for its future clinical applications. Furthermore, efforts should be made to promote the in vivo animal studies with CDs based formulations into clinical studies for their extensive exploration as a therapeutic delivery and imaging system. Over the last decade CDs application in drug delivery and imaging has been mainly focused on cancer therapy which could be expanded to other areas like in bone specific imaging and drug delivery, in Alzheimer’s disease and other neurodegenerative diseases.

In current scenario cancer has become a global concern in human health. Conventional chemotherapy in cancer disease brings inefficient therapeutic benefit along with lots of undesired side effects. Thus, continuous research for the advancement in the area of anticancer drug delivery is the need of the hour. Tremendous research progress in the area of nanotechnology has already been seen and nanoparticles have emerged as a promising approach for the successful development of nano-based drug delivery systems that could mitigate the unintended side effects and provide desirable therapeutic effects through selective delivery of drugs into cancer cells. In this respect, carbon nanoparticles especially CDs have gained tremendous attention over the last two decade, which simultaneously serves as a suitable drug carrier and a bioimaging probe. The excellent fluorescence property of CDs offers real time tracking of the movements of drug delivery system inside the body. Besides, CDs are reported to be nontoxic, biocompatible and to have several functional groups on their surface which help in successful conjugation of drugs and ligand molecules to develop suitable cancer targeted delivery systems. CDs have been widely used to develop cancer targeted imaging and delivery systems implementing several targeting strategies including passive targeting, active targeting and tumor microenvironment targeting. Several newer approaches are also under consideration like-multifunctional targeting, Nucleus and organelle targeting, etc.

A great progress has been made in the research of CDs based bio applications. However, there are some problems that need to be addressed for further advancements. The synthesis of CDs with uniform size is a major concern. The size distribution plays a vital role in determining the fluorescence property of CDs which has a direct impact on its bio application especially in vivo. The reproducibility of CDs synthesis is another big concern for its application. CDs can be prepared from numerous starting materials via several methods. Although, in each event difference is seen in their fluorescence property, size and other properties. Even, CDs produced in different batches using same method shows inconsistency in quantum yield (QY), size, etc. which restricts their commercialization. Low QY is another big challenge in the applications of CDs. Thus, further attempts should be made to find an efficient and reproducible approach to obtain uniform sized CDs and with improved QY. Better understanding of CDs intrinsic structure, surface functionality and underlying luminescence mechanism could provide further improvements for its application in bioimaging, drug delivery and other related fields. Besides, for the future development of CDs, the requirement of high wavelength emission emphasized in the red/ near IR region for effective tissue penetration in deep tissue imaging should be taken in consideration.

Thus, wise selection of starting materials and synthetic conditions to develop uniform sized CDs of desired fluorescence property and functional groups, implementation of suitable targeting strategies could provide tracing and selective targeting towards cancer and desired therapeutic efficacy.
Pradip Jana: Investigation, Writing − original draft preparation, Formal analysis. Abhimanyu Dev: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision, Visualization, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Pradip Jana would like to acknowledge Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), New Delhi, India for providing Senior Research Fellowship (SRF) (Award letter file no. 5/3/8/26/ITR-F/2022).
